speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying that there were some informers that you knew. What happened to the people that they informed on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite a few were arrested by the Germans and ended up in concentration camps. And a few didn't even come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the consequences were rather serious.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're very serious. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to these informers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- when they found out that they were informers, a couple of guys, they invite them to come to a-- in a farm. They say there was some American pilots hiding. And when they were there, they shot them. One was killed. The other-- the other was injured. And then the third one fled, and went betraying to the Germans, and tell them what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were you personally involved in that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was the time I was-- I was already in-- in prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. You heard about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard about it, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. Slightly different question. How did you begin your involvement with the Jews that you saved?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friend, Eric Van [? Assan, ?] he was an elder in our church. And he was an auditor with the income tax department. He knew quite a few Jewish people, businesspeople. And one day he approached me. And because I was a traveling salesman I had a pass on the train. He can go wherever you want. And he asked me if I was able to accompany a Jewish couple to his sister-in-law in Apeldoorn, where they were going to be in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell the name of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Apeldoorn is A-P-E-L-D-O-O-R-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in the center of Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the center. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I did that. I accompanied them, and drive them to his family. And afterward he came back. He [? post ?] me again. And he said, well, I have a friend who is an inspector of police force in Maastricht police office. And the police office has a Jewish family-- they were a new family-- a widow with two teenage kids. And [INAUDIBLE] look them for a hiding place. And he asked me I could find a hiding place. And it was-- the name of the family was Freilich, F-R-E-I-L-I-C-H. And the mother, she was a widow. And the daughter, Toni, and the son, Fritz. And that made me more or less active in finding a hiding place for-- for them. I found a hiding place for the mother, the daughter in a grocery store. I knew also members of our church in Haelen, about 30 kilometers from Maastricht.

speaker: interviewee
text: And but for him, for Fritz I had nothing. And his mother begged me to find something for [INAUDIBLE] him. And I asked my mother, I says, OK that Fritz comes for a couple of days at our place until I find a place for him? And my parents agreed to that. So Fritz came to our place for three or four days. And after that, my mom said, ah, he's such a nice fellow. Let him stay here, and we don't have to go anymore. And Fritz stayed at our place until the end of the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And through Fritz, I got more connections. His-- his cousin was in the hospital. And he was released from the hospital and needed a place. I found also a place for her. He brought me in contact with his uncle and his wife. And slowly on, like a snowball, more and more people approached me for finding a hiding place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time when this began, when you started to rescue Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was 22 years of age, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first reaction to the request?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt obliged to find a place for them. Because the consequences were if you couldn't find a place, they would end up in camp, and might-- might be killed, or murdered, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did that sense of obligation come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: It came from our vision in our church that we had to help those people who were in need. And we had-- we always felt-- was very sympathetic to the Jewish people for the simple reason we studied the Old Testament. So we know that they were people of the covenant, and that they [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So then it came partly through your religious--

speaker: interviewee
text: Through my religious upbringing, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Upbringing, and beliefs, and convictions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Christians who help people who are in need. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was part your value system?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. When Fritz stayed with you, was he just like part of the family? Did he move about? Did he go shopping with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He had to stay at the home. He couldn't go outside at all. We had open houses. Lots of people drop in at our place. And we want to keep-- to keep on doing it. Otherwise people are going to be suspicious.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had to go into hiding every time somebody knocked at the door and came into our house. He had to even to go to a little cellar, or upstairs in a small bedroom. Otherwise, he was part of the family. So only when-- when outsiders may have seen him--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --he was hiding. Yes. And he [INAUDIBLE]. Yes. And when he was sort of safe, he was just a member of the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were sitting [? with ?] his games together at night, you know. And yeah, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was Fritz at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the same age as I was. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: About 22?

speaker: interviewee
text: 22 years. Right. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. Do you remember the reaction of your parents when you asked them if Fritz could stay with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I asked first my mother, and she said, I have to discuss with Father. And then she said, well, it's only for a few days. We don't mind. But after the course [INAUDIBLE] Fritz [INAUDIBLE]. So it wasn't a problem anymore. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of person was Fritz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice guy, who was very flexible and had no problem adjusting to our family lifestyle. So he peeled for my mom the potatoes. He did the dish washing, and so on, and so on. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your work over the years, did you find that in some cases it didn't work out nearly as well as it did with Fritz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Majority worked out, yes. And no problems there. There was sometimes there was stress. And it didn't click. And then you had to look for a different place. But the majority I had no problems with. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: In those few instances where it didn't click, and there was stress, did you understand why that was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. You know, some personalities you are not easygoing, you know. Some-- some people want to-- didn't want to adjust to the lifestyle when the people were in the house where they were. You know, that is the problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: That must've been difficult for all-- everyone concerned.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyone concerned. I mean, it is not only with Jewish people. That happens with every family that moves together in one house. You know, you always get some stress and friction, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when there was that stress and friction, did they bring that problem to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. I-- it was my-- I used to visit them once a month to deliver the-- the food stamps. And then we sometimes picked up some mail for to be in contact with the family. And if they had some problems, they asked me. They say, well, is it possible that you find a different place for him? Because we cannot get along. But it didn't happen that often. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reaction in those cases?

speaker: interviewee
text: Usually, if I could find a place, then I did that. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find it unusual that some of these people had a hard time adjusting?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not at all. I mean it's quite [INAUDIBLE] in the event for people who have-- well-to-do people who live in a big house, and all of a sudden they had to go in a small, [INAUDIBLE] hiding place. That gives stress.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that you had an understanding--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for their difficulties. What was your relationship with the people that you saved prior to the war? Did you have any relationship?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't know them them. I didn't know them then.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were strangers to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: All strangers to me, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that didn't prevent you from doing what you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm. In finding hiding places, and in helping these people with food, or ID cards, or ration coupons, and so on, who did you have to deal with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I set up a scheme to have those food stamps. First, I started Fritz. We have a master card-- a master ID card for food stamps. We got those already before the war set up with Dutch Goldman in case a war was coming. And then the Master Card you had to go to a local office-- usually in schools or community halls. And you had to take the master card along. And he put a little stamp in the master card. And on the stamp you got a sheet with food stamps for a whole month. Now I, as you know in Holland, is-- everybody had to go, if he change his address, to City Hall. And it's very strict. If he don't do that, then you get fined with that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what I did, I checked and looked at how City Hall did that. And the only date in the master card, he changed the address, and put a [INAUDIBLE] behind, it and that was it. No stamp. So I did it with Fritz myself. I changed his address to not my address, but another address, and changed in my neighborhood, and put [INAUDIBLE] behind it. And then I tried it out. I went to one of these bureaus, and yeah, yeah, I got them, sheets, food stamps for him as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I did it also for his mother, and for his sister, and for his cousin. And slowly on. And a whole bunch of master cards. But I couldn't have been to one office. So he went from one to the other. Two there, and then three there, you know. And in that way. Because if they didn't have food stamps, it was hard for the people where you're were hiding. They had to go to the black market and pay big amounts of money for that. And that way they could come the normal market, buy food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that dangerous to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very dangerous, of course. I-- I had to-- it was a kind of risk I took. But I had no problems with that. Beginning it was only a few. And then, as people knew that I found a source to find food stamps-- food stamps-- I got a whole bunch of cards, and ended up with more than 100. And then it became dangerous. So I found somebody in, you know, head office in Haelan. I found somebody that was willing to do that at night, change it over. And that was-- and then after that, they grow again. And in the end, before he was arrested. And there were 300 people who looked out to the food stamps.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way was it dangerous? What could have happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well you could-- they could have arrested me, or they could search me in the train, or whatever. They could find those. And they-- at the least he could have said, you're on the black market. And he could arrest me for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And despite those dangers, you continued to do the work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I continued to do that, until the man in the head office, from the office in Haelen, got scared, too. And then he asked me the next time when I visit him again, he said, I handed it over to a police officer. And there was inspector of the police force in Haelen. And he took care of that every month. I had to go to his head bureau, picked up the food stamps, and hand them out to all the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of the people who you helped, did any of those offer you money, or-- or jewelry, or some sort of incentive for you to do what you were doing? No. Not at all, no. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you request any compensation from these people, and form of payment for what you were doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was there a time when the money that you used to-- to do what you were doing ran short?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. We-- sometimes Jewish people who were in hiding who were well-to-do, and had money available, they gave some donation for those Jewish people who couldn't afford that. And we had our church. We had funds for helping people in-- who were in hiding. So and I got this from the church funds as well. And so it came from some wealthy Jews, but also from your church?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Special funds that were allocated for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yes. Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you hide the Jews that you had rescued?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, usually I went to the minister of our church in-- in a place somewhere in the southern part of Holland, and I ask him if he knew anybody in his congregation who are willing to hide Jews. And a couple days later I visit him again. And he introduced me to some-- two family who were willing to do that. And they could do that, of course, because it was known that in our church, you know, they were all against national [? socialism. ?] So we end up going there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- your contact person was someone in the church?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yes. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you networked through the members of the church.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. How did you get food for the people in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just told you. Through the food stamps. And I-- [INAUDIBLE] brought us the sheet every month to these-- to these people. And they could just at the normal market buy-- buy food and whatever was available.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by providing the food stamps, they then--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yes. Yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --could go on their own. OK. All right. OK Did the Jews themselves go? Or were the people who were hiding them? It was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. The people who were hiding them, they bought the food. They-- they couldn't go outside. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they-- was someone never suspicious if they came and bought more food than they normally did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have never heard about that. It might be, but I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing ever came of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone ever know that you were involved in the hiding of these Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only a small group. A small group, they knew about it. But the majority didn't know that, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the members of the small group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well it was member of our church, and people I have contact with through an illegal magazine. I was a representative for that. So I handed those people already beforehand 20 or 50 copies of that-- of that magazine, and got to know them, that they were-- I could trust them. And then through them I could find other places. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of precautions did you take so that other people who might harm the program wouldn't find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, like I say, I was using a different name, the name of Alexander. I usually told people that they were not living in my state, or even in another city. A couple times I had to transport a Jewish person-- persons from the railroad station to-- to my house for a couple of nights. And then when they walked from the railroad station to my house, instead of the north-- taking the normal normal route-- I took them and go all different route. And so that they could hardly could find a way back-- way back-- in case something would happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many details that you had to keep in mind, in terms of protecting yourself and those other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. They had to have-- they had to have also an ID card, because you couldn't use their own ID card with a J on it. So we had to falsified stolen ID cards. And then a friend who was a student in high school, he pick-pocket coats from girls and men. They usually had the ID card in their pocket. Because it was easier for the people to go to the police officer and say something stole my ID card, and you were honest about it. And that way we had somebody at the police office who was an artist. And he changed the picture and copied the stamp exactly like it was before. And they used that for the time being to identify themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone ever come so close to figuring out what you were doing that they tried to blackmail you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard my sister had problems when I was already arrested, that she got a note in the mail. And the note said, we know you have a Jewish boy at home. And we warn you. And then they took Fritz away for a couple of-- couple of weeks-- to a friend of mine. And then he came back, and nothing happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- was that a help, or was it a warning?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a warning. But we had-- later on we got a feeling that it was somebody who was-- want to protect us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So that it wasn't a form of blackmail.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not at all. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when those people-- the Jews in hiding-- needed some medical attention? How was that handled?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the people who were when they were in hiding, they used to go to the [NON-ENGLISH], family doctor. I usually could trust him. So there was no problem there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they go?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people couldn't go. The doctor came at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, made house calls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Made house calls. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. And someone notified the doctor that they needed some help.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's right. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any unusual medical circumstances that came up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I know one occasion the Jewish lady was pregnant, and she got a baby at home. And you could cover it up with the doctor. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the doctor present for that birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's right. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a member of any organized resistance group?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a member originally from the organization Free Netherlands, to which [INAUDIBLE] the magazine. But for helping Jewish and other people who were been brought into hiding, it was my own set up. Later on, of course, we borrowed in-- into a large organization in Netherlands called LO. And there we got more financial support, and we got more food stamps, yes, through them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does LO stand for?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in the National Organization of Helping People in Hiding, yeah. And it was a broad organization. It was not only-- not only for Jewish, but all the political prisoners who had to go in hiding. And boys who had to go to work in Germany, they are also hiding, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that there were different periods of time in your activity. And at the beginning, you were sort of the whole operation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's right. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just you on your own.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Later on, you joined this other organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did you receive any assistance from the resistance group, or-- or anyone else in doing your work? My sister helped me with doing some work for the-- for hiding Jews. And some friends [INAUDIBLE] knows about it. Which sister are you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marg-- Margareta. Margareta. My younger sister, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And the friends that you've referred to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a friend named John Bosch, who found places for me for Jewish children. And he extended that quite a bit. So yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell his name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's John Bosch, B-O-S-C-H. He was just last year honored at Yad Vashem.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What other activities were the resistance groups involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, later on they-- they became more organized. And they were involved in burglarizing the offices for food stamps. They attacked sometimes German soldiers, or killed traitors.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the resistance groups recruit new members to join?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one-- you know, one brought in another friend, slowly on. You know, you treat it like a snowball, until you get more people you can trust, and you know who they stand for.

speaker: interviewer
text: So more and more people came forward.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the group know who they could contact in order to shelter the Jews? How did they know who they could trust, and who they could not trust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, we didn't-- we didn't-- usually we had a-- a piece of paper, and ripped that in two pieces, and gave one guy one of the piece, and then the man is coming, we show the other piece. He can trust him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That way we introduce our self, usually.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that there was no confusion as to whose side they were on.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: If they had half the sheet of paper--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. OK. You mentioned earlier that there was an instance where some of the collaborators were taken out to the farm and killed. Was this the only instance where something like that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I know. In the northern part of Holland more of those things happened. But that's only thing I am aware of. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you personally involved in anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was already in prison at that time. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] You mentioned earlier that the church was involved in helping to find homes for the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Members of the church, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they go about doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the minister would approach some people that he thought, well, they might have a bigger house. They can easily have some people in hiding. And that way he informed me about it. And then I visit those people, told them about the dangers, and all that. You know. And-- but they usually agreed to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned earlier that you were able to get additional ration cards by some forging.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you also help people get forged identity cards?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct. First of all, I did it on the way, as a told you, by pick-pocketing people, and exchanging that. But later on, there were [? complete ?] printing officers in Amsterdam. And I got connection with them. And then he copied the original ID card, and make it exactly the way it looked before. And that way, even better of course. Because the-- the stolen ones, they came on a big list. And the numbers on the list that they are stolen. And you're lucky there were no computers at that time. Because it could take a long time before they found out what number it was. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you move about from city to city without creating suspicion that you were doing something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the-- well, for several years a traveling salesman. First I worked in-- for a chemical company. And I sold [INAUDIBLE] tea, and coffee, and those things to stores. When I was finished was when I, you know, I have nothing to do anyone. Then I joining a company for who sold office machines. So it was known that I was a traveling salesman. And when I quit there, because my work for hiding people was too much, I still carry on that name, and people interacted [? train ?] with me-- [? with ?] me. And [? a sample ?] case, and so on and so on. You just actively watch [INAUDIBLE] when you're working.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as far as anyone was concerned, you were still a traveling salesman?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you quit, or were you asked to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I discussed with the manager. And I said, well, you have to make a choice. Either you work full-time for us, or you're doing other things. And I decided to quit, and to keep on working for helping people who were in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you gave up your paying job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I gave up paying job. That's right. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: To do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Later on, in the resistance group, were there-- were there children involved? I mean, were children actually doing resistance work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Groceries. We used girls for delivering food stamps, because girls are not arrested. If you are a man at 18, 19, 20, they would pick you up and send you to full work in Germany. So they were-- they can easy do something than the men are doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the girls weren't treated the same way as-- as the boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's right. Yes. Of course, for them there was no danger to be picked up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it easy to appeal to the young women to do that kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yeah. No problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were quite willing?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were quite willing, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were as courageous as the boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yes. For example, I got-- I had conflict in Amsterdam. And the family, they asked me-- two friend, two student-- if they could visit them. And they had a little baby who was 12 days old. And they asked me for a hiding place for the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went back to Maastricht by train. And I couldn't find a-- a place right away. So we asked my parents if it was OK that the baby could stay at our place for a couple days until we find a place. And my mom agreed to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then took a girl along with me, went back to Amsterdam, pick up the baby in a little basket. And acting as a married couple we went back to Maastricht. And the baby came to my parents' place. And after a few days, my mom said-- my mom said, we like the baby so much. We can stay here. Stay there till the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in both cases, when you brought Fritz, it was only for a few days. And your mother said--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --well, let him stay. And you brought this 11-day-old baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, the baby can stay as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can stay as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a Jewish baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish baby. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who looked after that infant?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister did. And Fritz did part of duties. He gave the bottle, and whatever that he could do, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that something dangerous? Not giving the bottle, but having an 11-day-old baby in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My sister bleached the baby's hair. It was black. And we told the neighborhood that one them, my aunt was-- had TB, and was in the hospital, and asked us to look after the baby till she was back out of the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you let the other people think that it was a member of the family--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the mother was in the sanitary--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my sister walk around with her. And it was like a normal baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: With dyed hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: With dyed hair. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Did-- did anyone make any comments about the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the train, and the-- the girl I had with me, and there was even a German soldier. He looked at it for a cute baby. And somebody on the train said it looked like me, the father. And another said it looked like the girl. It looked like the mother. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they made comparisons--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and one said they looked like the father, and the other like the mother. Interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with the Gestapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any run-ins with the Gestapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had [INAUDIBLE] was arrested.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were arrested. OK. We'll probably get to that in a little while. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What-- what did your family think about the activities that you were involved in, in view of the fact that you were placing yourself in danger, as well as them, as well as the Jewish people you were hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents encouraged me to do that. They never-- we were-- we were scared, of course. [INAUDIBLE] just got normal. But they encouraged me to do that. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they never had any hesitation about getting involved in that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not at all. No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever talk to you about what you did after the war, in terms of the-- the danger that they were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course you were in danger. But they never-- they never had somebody who searched the house. And they never had a suspicion that they were involved. Only when I was interrogated by the Gestapo when I was arrested, he tried to blackmail me more or less to tell me that my father was also arrested. And I was [INAUDIBLE] for that, because I took my own responsibility for that, my parents. And he said, your father's arrested, he said. And he said he's in same prison where I was. And then I-- a couple of days later, because I was very afraid of that, a couple days later I got a parcel through the Red Cross from home. And the handwriting of the parcel is my father. And that way I could play with the Germans, and acted if it was-- that I believed that. But I knew it was not true.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll take a break now and we'll continue in just a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
